examples.title: Algorithm: Circular Queue Operations
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating enqueue, dequeue, isEmpty, and isFull operations on a circular queue using modular arithmetic
examples.code:
ALGORITHM CircularQueueOperations
INPUT: queue array Q[0..MAXSIZE-1], capacity N, operations to perform
OUTPUT: Results of queue operations

// Data Structure Definition
STRUCTURE CircularQueue
 items[0..MAXSIZE-1] // Array to store elements
 front // Index of front element
 rear // Index of rear element
 capacity // Maximum size of queue
ENDSTRUCTURE

// Initialize Queue
ALGORITHM Initialize(Q, capacity)
 Q.capacity ← capacity
 Q.front ← -1
 Q.rear ← -1
 return Q
ENDALGORITHM

// Check if Queue is Empty
ALGORITHM isEmpty(Q)
 IF Q.front = -1 AND Q.rear = -1 THEN
 return TRUE
 ELSE
 return FALSE
 ENDIF
ENDALGORITHM

// Check if Queue is Full
ALGORITHM isFull(Q)
 // Queue is full when next rear position equals front
 IF (Q.rear + 1) MOD Q.capacity = Q.front THEN
 return TRUE
 ELSE
 return FALSE
 ENDIF
ENDALGORITHM

// Enqueue Operation - Add element to rear
ALGORITHM Enqueue(Q, value)
 IF isFull(Q) THEN
 PRINT "Queue Overflow - Queue is full"
 return ERROR
 ENDIF
 
 // If queue is empty, initialize both pointers
 IF isEmpty(Q) THEN
 Q.front ← 0
 Q.rear ← 0
 ELSE
 // Circular increment of rear using modulo
 Q.rear ← (Q.rear + 1) MOD Q.capacity
 ENDIF
 
 // Insert element at rear position
 Q.items[Q.rear] ← value
 PRINT "Enqueued: ", value
 return SUCCESS
ENDALGORITHM

// Dequeue Operation - Remove element from front
ALGORITHM Dequeue(Q)
 IF isEmpty(Q) THEN
 PRINT "Queue Underflow - Queue is empty"
 return ERROR
 ENDIF
 
 // Store the front element to return
 item ← Q.items[Q.front]
 
 // If only one element exists, reset queue
 IF Q.front = Q.rear THEN
 Q.front ← -1
 Q.rear ← -1
 ELSE
 // Circular increment of front using modulo
 Q.front ← (Q.front + 1) MOD Q.capacity
 ENDIF
 
 PRINT "Dequeued: ", item
 return item
ENDALGORITHM

// Peek - View front element without removing
ALGORITHM Peek(Q)
 IF isEmpty(Q) THEN
 PRINT "Queue is empty"
 return ERROR
 ENDIF
 return Q.items[Q.front]
ENDALGORITHM

// Example Usage Trace
ALGORITHM Main
 Q ← Initialize(5)
 
 // Trace: Enqueue 10, 20, 30
 Enqueue(Q, 10) // front=0, rear=0
 Enqueue(Q, 20) // front=0, rear=1
 Enqueue(Q, 30) // front=0, rear=2
 
 // Trace: Dequeue twice
 Dequeue(Q) // Returns 10, front=1, rear=2
 Dequeue(Q) // Returns 20, front=2, rear=2
 
 // Trace: Enqueue 40, 50, 60 (wraps around)
 Enqueue(Q, 40) // front=2, rear=3
 Enqueue(Q, 50) // front=2, rear=4
 Enqueue(Q, 60) // front=2, rear=0 (wraps!), Queue FULL
ENDALGORITHM
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the core operations of a circular queue. Key points: 1) The modulo operator (MOD) enables circular wrapping of indices, 2) isFull uses (rear+1)%capacity == front to check fullness while sacrificing one slot, 3) isEmpty returns true only when both front and rear are -1, 4) When dequeuing the last element, both pointers reset to -1 to indicate empty state.

examples.title: Python: Circular Queue Implementation
examples.description: Complete Python implementation of a circular queue with all operations and demonstration of wrap-around behavior
examples.code:
"""
Circular Queue Implementation in Python
2022 Scheme - Data Structures Topic: Circular Queues

This implementation demonstrates all queue operations with proper
handling of circular increment using modulo arithmetic.
"""

class CircularQueue:
 """
 A Circular Queue implementation using array representation.
 Sacrifices one slot to distinguish between full and empty states.
 """
 
 def __init__(self, capacity):
 """
 Initialize the circular queue with given capacity.
 Note: Maximum elements stored will be (capacity - 1)
 """
 self.capacity = capacity
 self.queue = [None] * capacity # Array to store elements
 self.front = -1 # Index of front element
 self.rear = -1 # Index of rear element
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """Check if queue is empty"""
 return self.front == -1 and self.rear == -1
 
 def is_full(self):
 """Check if queue is full using circular increment logic"""
 # Queue is full when next rear position equals front
 return (self.rear + 1) % self.capacity == self.front
 
 def enqueue(self, value):
 """
 Add an element to the rear of the queue.
 Uses circular increment: rear = (rear + 1) % capacity
 """
 if self.is_full():
 print(f"Queue Overflow! Cannot enqueue {value}. Queue is full.")
 return False
 
 # If queue is empty, initialize both pointers
 if self.is_empty():
 self.front = 0
 self.rear = 0
 else:
 # Circular increment - wraps around using modulo
 self.rear = (self.rear + 1) % self.capacity
 
 self.queue[self.rear] = value
 print(f"Enqueued {value} | Front={self.front}, Rear={self.rear}")
 return True
 
 def dequeue(self):
 """
 Remove and return the front element from the queue.
 Uses circular increment: front = (front + 1) % capacity
 """
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue Underflow! Queue is empty.")
 return None
 
 # Store the front element
 item = self.queue[self.front]
 self.queue[self.front] = None # Optional: clear the slot
 
 # If only one element existed, reset queue to empty
 if self.front == self.rear:
 self.front = -1
 self.rear = -1
 else:
 # Circular increment - wraps around using modulo
 self.front = (self.front + 1) % self.capacity
 
 print(f"Dequeued {item} | Front={self.front}, Rear={self.rear}")
 return item
 
 def peek(self):
 """Return front element without removing it"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue is empty.")
 return None
 return self.queue[self.front]
 
 def get_rear(self):
 """Return rear element without removing it"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue is empty.")
 return None
 return self.queue[self.rear]
 
 def display(self):
 """Display the current state of the queue"""
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Queue: EMPTY")
 return
 
 print("Queue State:")
 print(f" Front index: {self.front}")
 print(f" Rear index: {self.rear}")
 print(f" Contents: ", end="")
 
 # Traverse circularly to display all elements
 if self.front <= self.rear:
 # Normal case: front <= rear
 for i in range(self.front, self.rear + 1):
 print(f"[{self.queue[i]}]", end=" ")
 else:
 # Wrap-around case: front > rear
 for i in range(self.front, self.capacity):
 print(f"[{self.queue[i]}]", end=" ")
 for i in range(0, self.rear + 1):
 print(f"[{self.queue[i]}]", end=" ")
 print("\n")


def demonstrate_circular_queue():
 """
 Demonstrate circular queue operations with step-by-step trace.
 This pattern is frequently asked in university examinations.
 """
 print("=" * 60)
 print("CIRCULAR QUEUE DEMONSTRATION")
 print("=" * 60)
 
 # Create queue of capacity 5 (can store max 4 elements)
 cq = CircularQueue(5)
 
 print("\n--- Step 1: Initial State ---")
 cq.display()
 print(f"isEmpty: {cq.is_empty()}, isFull: {cq.is_full()}")
 
 print("\n--- Step 2: Enqueue 10, 20, 30 ---")
 cq.enqueue(10) # front=0, rear=0
 cq.enqueue(20) # front=0, rear=1
 cq.enqueue(30) # front=0, rear=2
 cq.display()
 
 print("\n--- Step 3: Dequeue twice ---")
 cq.dequeue() # Returns 10, front becomes 1
 cq.dequeue() # Returns 20, front becomes 2
 cq.display()
 print(f"Notice: Space at indices 0, 1 will be reused!")
 
 print("\n--- Step 4: Enqueue 40, 50, 60 (demonstrates wrap-around) ---")
 cq.enqueue(40) # rear becomes 3
 cq.enqueue(50) # rear becomes 4
 cq.enqueue(60) # rear wraps to 0! Queue becomes full
 cq.display()
 print(f"isEmpty: {cq.is_empty()}, isFull: {cq.is_full()}")
 
 print("\n--- Step 5: Try to enqueue when full ---")
 cq.enqueue(70) # Should fail - queue overflow
 
 print("\n--- Step 6: Peek and get_rear ---")
 print(f"Front element (peek): {cq.peek()}")
 print(f"Rear element: {cq.get_rear()}")
 
 print("\n--- Step 7: Dequeue all remaining elements ---")
 while not cq.is_empty():
 cq.dequeue()
 cq.display()


if __name__ == "__main__":
 demonstrate_circular_queue()

 # Print complexity analysis
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("TIME COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS")
 print("=" * 60)
 print("All operations are O(1) - Constant Time")
 print(" - enqueue(): O(1)")
 print(" - dequeue(): O(1)")
 print(" - isEmpty(): O(1)")
 print(" - isFull(): O(1)")
 print(" - peek(): O(1)")
 print("Space Complexity: O(n) where n = capacity")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates a complete circular queue with all required operations. Key implementation details: 1) Uses a fixed-size list with modular arithmetic for circular increment, 2) Sacrifices one slot (capacity-1 max elements) to distinguish full from empty states, 3) The isFull condition uses (rear+1)%capacity == front, 4) Display method handles both normal and wrap-around traversal. The demonstration shows the wrap-around behavior which is crucial for university exams.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is the capacity

examples.title: C: Circular Queue Implementation
examples.description: Complete C implementation of circular queue with array representation, demonstrating enqueue, dequeue, and status checking operations
examples.explanation: This C implementation provides a complete circular queue with array representation. Key aspects: 1) Uses #define MAXSIZE to set capacity, 2) isFull uses modular arithmetic: (rear+1)%capacity == front, 3) Enqueue handles first element case specially, 4) Dequeue resets both pointers when only one element exists, 5) Display function handles both normal and wrap-around traversal. The main function demonstrates a complete sequence similar to university exam questions.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) - All operations are constant time
